target.title: fix(matrix): honor MSC3967 and surface MAS reset URL on cross-signing bootstrap
target.body:
# Honor MSC3967 first-publish and surface MAS reset stage cleanly when cross-signing requires UIA

## What this PR does

Makes the Matrix channel's cross-signing bootstrap survive Synapse homeservers fronted by Matrix Authentication Service (MSC3861, e.g. ESS deployments) when the bot logs in with a Personal Access Token. Today those bots fail with a misleading "provide `matrix.password` for `m.login.password` fallback" error and never publish cross-signing keys, breaking E2EE message decryption and self-verification.

After this change:

- A bot with **no** server-side master cross-signing key publishes a fresh identity using the [MSC3967](https://github.com/matrix-org/matrix-spec-proposals/pull/3967) first-publish path (no UIA, no auth callback round trip).
- A bot whose local secret-storage state is stale relative to the server gets a typed `MatrixCrossSigningResetRequiredError` carrying the MAS reset URL, so the operator can complete the OAuth re-auth in a browser and re-run the bootstrap inside the MAS approval window.
- The UIA callback now inspects the 401 response body, routes by *advertised* stage rather than blindly trying `m.login.dummy` then `m.login.password`, and threads the UIA `session` token through subsequent requests (matrix-js-sdk's `authCallback` contract requires this).

Bug report with full reproduction steps and environment details: [MAS-CROSSSIGNING-BUG-REPORT.md](./MAS-CROSSSIGNING-BUG-REPORT.md) — should be filed as the companion issue.

## Changes

### `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk/types.ts`

- New exported error classes:
  - `MatrixCrossSigningResetRequiredError(stages, resetUrl?, session?)` — thrown when the homeserver demands `org.matrix.cross_signing_reset`. Message includes the MAS URL when advertised, and a fallback note pointing at the Synapse admin reset endpoint when no URL is provided.
  - `MatrixUiaUnsupportedStagesError(stages, hasPassword)` — thrown when the homeserver advertises only stages this client cannot satisfy non-interactively (e.g. `m.login.password` on a passwordless PAT-only bot).
- New types: `MatrixHomeserverCapabilities`, `MatrixUiaResponseBody`.

### `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk/crypto-bootstrap.ts`

- `MatrixCryptoBootstrapperDeps` gains an optional `getHomeserverCapabilities?: () => Promise<MatrixHomeserverCapabilities>`.
- New `getHomeserverCapabilitiesCached()` / `isMasFronted()` / `masResetActionUrl()` helpers (the last constructs `<account_management_uri>?action=org.matrix.cross_signing_reset` per MSC2965 §6.4.4).
- New `isUiaChallenge()` / `extractUiaStages()` / `extractMasResetUrl()` / `isCrossSigningKeyMismatchError()` parser helpers (private to the file).
- `createSigningKeysUiAuthCallback` rewritten:
  - First attempt is always `makeRequest(null)` (the MSC3967 first-publish path).
  - If that throws a 401-with-UIA-body, parse the advertised stages.
  - Route by stage: `m.login.dummy` → `{type, session}`; `m.login.password` (with configured password) → `{type, identifier, password, session}`; `org.matrix.cross_signing_reset` → `MatrixCrossSigningResetRequiredError` carrying URL (from the 401 body if present, else constructed via MSC2965 from the cached `account_management_uri`) + session.
  - On MAS-fronted homeservers without a satisfiable stage, also surface `MatrixCrossSigningResetRequiredError`. On non-MAS, throw `MatrixUiaUnsupportedStagesError`.
  - All other 401 bodies and non-401 errors re-throw unchanged.
- `bootstrapCrossSigning()` keeps the existing first-pass / forced-reset / fallback-reset structure, with two changes:
  - Every catch block now propagates `MatrixCrossSigningResetRequiredError` instances as-is rather than wrapping/logging them, so the actionable URL reaches the CLI cleanly.
  - The bootstrapper deliberately **does not** short-circuit before the upload attempt on MAS. A reset upload to MAS looks doomed (Synapse 401s with the cross-signing-reset stage), but when the operator has just approved that action in MAS via a browser, the next upload **within the approval window** succeeds. Letting the upload happen in both states means the same code path serves both the "tell me what to do" and "do it" sides of recovery — the 401 turns into a structured error with the URL; the 200 lands cross-signing.

### `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk.ts`

- New `detectHomeserverCapabilities()` method that probes `/_matrix/client/v1/auth_metadata` and returns `{ msAuthService, accountManagementUri }`. `msAuthService` is true when the endpoint returns 200 with an OAuth `issuer`; `accountManagementUri` carries the MSC2965 base URL used to construct action URLs. Result is cached on the instance.
  - Note: an earlier draft used `unstable_features["org.matrix.msc3861"]` from `client.getVersions()`. That doesn't work — Synapse 1.145+/ESS no longer surfaces the unstable flag, so the only reliable in-band MAS marker is the auth_metadata endpoint itself.
- Wired into the `MatrixCryptoBootstrapper` deps in `ensureCryptoSupportInitialized()`.

## What this PR explicitly does **not** do

- Does **not** implement an OAuth re-auth flow inside the bot. The `org.matrix.cross_signing_reset` stage is designed to require a human in a browser; we surface the URL and stop. Operators who need fully unattended recovery should use Synapse's `POST /_synapse/admin/v1/users/<user>/_reset_cross_signing_keys` admin endpoint and then let the bot do a clean MSC3967 first-publish.
- Does **not** modify matrix-js-sdk. The UIA callback is the SDK's already-supported extensibility point; openclaw just wasn't using it correctly.
- Does **not** touch `account_data` to "reset" cross-signing — server-side cross-signing public keys live in a separate keys table queried via `/keys/query`, not in `account_data`, so clearing `m.cross_signing.{master,self_signing,user_signing}` would not unblock MSC3967. (An earlier draft of this patch tried that path and it was dropped after verification.)
- Does **not** change matrix-rust-sdk OTK regeneration behaviour, the `verify list/accept/start/sas/confirm-sas` gateway-RPC routing, or the Bonjour mDNS spam — those are separate issues.

## Verified end-to-end on real MAS-fronted Synapse

Confirmed against `matrix.thepolycule.ca` (Synapse 1.145.0+ess.1, MAS-fronted, OAuth issuer `https://auth.matrix.thepolycule.ca/`). With this patch the bot can now:

1. Detect MAS via `/_matrix/client/v1/auth_metadata` (status 200, OAuth issuer present).
2. On `verify bootstrap --force-reset-cross-signing` with no MAS approval current, attempt the reset upload, receive Synapse's 401, and surface `MatrixCrossSigningResetRequiredError` with the URL `https://auth.matrix.thepolycule.ca/account/?action=org.matrix.cross_signing_reset` (constructed via MSC2965 from the cached `account_management_uri`).
3. After the operator opens that URL and approves the cross-signing reset action in MAS, the next `verify bootstrap --force-reset-cross-signing` within the MAS approval window has its `/keys/device_signing/upload` succeed (200), and `/_matrix/client/v3/keys/query` thereafter returns the bot's freshly published `master_keys` and `self_signing_keys` for the first time.

Pre-existing matrix-rust-sdk OTK regeneration collisions on `/keys/upload` (`signed_curve25519:AAAAAAAAAA0 already exists`) and incomplete owner-side device verification are observable in the same e2e run but are unrelated to this PR — they are filed as separate upstream issues.

## Tests

`extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk/crypto-bootstrap.test.ts`:

- 2 existing UIA tests rewritten to use real 401-shaped error bodies (`{httpStatus: 401, data: {flows, session, params}}`) instead of generic `Error("need auth")`, matching the matrix-js-sdk MatrixError shape that the SDK actually delivers to `authCallback`.
- 5 new regression tests:
  - `uses dummy UIA stage when advertised even alongside other stages` — verifies stage prioritisation when both dummy and password are offered.
  - `surfaces MatrixCrossSigningResetRequiredError with the MAS reset URL` — asserts the error carries `resetUrl`, `session`, and `stages`.
  - `attempts forced reset on MAS so a freshly approved reset can land within the MAS window` — asserts the bootstrapper does NOT short-circuit; it attempts the upload so a current MAS approval can land cross-signing on the same call.
  - `allows MSC3967 first-publish on MAS when the user has no master key yet` — asserts no-auth path succeeds.
  - `falls through to a reset upload after an import-key mismatch on MAS, surfacing the MAS URL on 401` — asserts the post-mismatch fallback attempts a `setupNewCrossSigning` upload (so a recent MAS approval can land it) and surfaces `MatrixCrossSigningResetRequiredError` with the MSC2965-constructed URL when no approval is current.

`pnpm test --filter @openclaw/matrix` (or the upstream equivalent) — 26 tests in `crypto-bootstrap.test.ts` pass; full sdk-dir suite (5 files, 65 tests) passes.

## Test plan for reviewers

End-to-end against a Synapse 1.79+ deployment fronted by MAS (e.g. ESS) with a PAT-authenticated bot:

1. Create the bot account in MAS, mint a PAT, configure `channels.matrix.<account>.userId` and `MATRIX_ACCESS_TOKEN`, deploy.
2. Ensure server-side cross-signing is empty for the bot (use `POST /_synapse/admin/v1/users/<user>/_reset_cross_signing_keys` if not).
3. Run `openclaw matrix verify bootstrap`.
   - Expected: `POST .../keys/device_signing/upload [... 200]` (MSC3967 path).
   - Expected: `PUT .../room_keys/version [... 200]` (key backup created).
4. Run `openclaw matrix verify status` — should show `Cross-signing published: yes`.
5. Negative test: corrupt the bot's local secret storage `m.cross_signing.master` blob and re-run `verify bootstrap`. Expected: clean exit with `MatrixCrossSigningResetRequiredError`, MAS URL printed, no silent retry loop.
6. Encrypted DM round-trip from a verified Element X session — expect successful Megolm decrypt in the bot log and a decrypted reply on the sender's client.

## Related

- Bug report: [MAS-CROSSSIGNING-BUG-REPORT.md](./MAS-CROSSSIGNING-BUG-REPORT.md)
- [MSC3967 — Do not require UIA when first uploading cross-signing keys](https://github.com/matrix-org/matrix-spec-proposals/pull/3967)
- [MSC3861 — Next-generation auth for Matrix](https://github.com/matrix-org/matrix-spec-proposals/pull/3861)
- [Synapse PR #17509 — `org.matrix.cross_signing_reset` UIA stage for MAS](https://github.com/element-hq/synapse/pull/17509)

target.title: feat(openai): add queryTask/documentTask config for Jina v5 task-specific embeddings
target.body:
## Summary

Add typed `queryTask` and `documentTask` configuration under `agents.*.memorySearch` for Jina v5 task-specific embeddings through the OpenAI-compatible embedding provider.

Closes #74419

## Background

Jina Embeddings v5 supports task-specific LoRA adapters via a `task` request-body parameter. Without this config, OpenClaw can call the model but cannot select query/document-optimized adapters such as `retrieval.query` and `retrieval.passage`.

## Changes

- Add `queryTask` and `documentTask` to memory search config, zod schema, resolved config, plugin/SDK embedding option types, labels/help text, and generated config baseline hash.
- Forward `queryTask` on inline query embeddings and `documentTask` on document/batch embedding requests in the OpenAI embedding provider.
- Include `documentTask` in OpenAI batch embedding cache identity.
- Document the Jina v5 task parameters in the OpenAI provider docs and memory config reference.
- Add targeted tests for config propagation, OpenAI request bodies, batch request bodies, and schema help coverage.

## Supported task values

- `retrieval.query` / `retrieval.passage` — asymmetric retrieval
- `text-matching` — symmetric semantic matching
- `clustering` — cluster-friendly embeddings
- `classification` — classification-friendly embeddings
- `separation` — separation-focused embeddings

When left unset, Jina v5 defaults to general-purpose embeddings.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** OpenClaw should emit Jina v5 `task` request-body parameters from `queryTask` for query embeddings and `documentTask` for document/indexing embeddings when using the OpenAI-compatible embedding provider.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout on Linux/Node 22, running the actual `extensions/openai/embedding-provider.ts` code against a local OpenAI-compatible HTTP capture server. The API key in the command setup was a redacted local dummy value and no external secrets were used.

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:** Ran `node --import tsx /tmp/jina-proof.mjs`. The script started a local `/v1/embeddings` capture server, created the OpenClaw OpenAI embedding provider with `model: "jina-embeddings-v3"`, `queryTask: "retrieval.query"`, and `documentTask: "retrieval.passage"`, then called `provider.embedQuery("query text")` and `provider.embedBatch(["document text"])`.

**Evidence after fix:** Live terminal output from the run:

```bash
$ node --import tsx /tmp/jina-proof.mjs
{
  "setup": "local OpenAI-compatible capture server + OpenClaw OpenAI embedding provider",
  "requests": [
    {
      "method": "POST",
      "url": "/v1/embeddings",
      "body": {
        "model": "jina-embeddings-v3",
        "input": [
          "query text"
        ],
        "task": "retrieval.query"
      }
    },
    {
      "method": "POST",
      "url": "/v1/embeddings",
      "body": {
        "model": "jina-embeddings-v3",
        "input": [
          "document text"
        ],
        "task": "retrieval.passage"
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

**Observed result after fix:** The captured OpenClaw-generated query embedding request included `task: "retrieval.query"`, and the captured document embedding request included `task: "retrieval.passage"`. This also confirms the reviewed batch/document source is the resolved client task value rather than stale raw options.

**What was not tested:** Live calls to the external Jina API were not made; the proof uses a local OpenAI-compatible capture server to verify OpenClaw's emitted request bodies without external credentials.

The same branch also passed the targeted validation listed in the PR comments.

target.title: fix: ensure CLI processes exit after command completion on Windows
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes CLI processes (node.exe openclaw.mjs) remaining alive after command execution on Windows. The CLI entrypoint was missing process.exit() calls after commands completed, causing processes to hang.

## Changes

- Added explicit `process.exit(process.exitCode ?? 0)` call after CLI entrypoint execution in openclaw.mjs
- Ensures all async code paths properly terminate on Windows

## Testing

- Verified CLI commands now exit cleanly on Windows after completion
- Existing test suite passes

Fixes openclaw/openclaw#74378

target.title: feat(browser): add agent download actions
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: the agent-facing `browser` tool did not expose the managed browser download helpers even though the CLI/control API already supported `/download` and `/wait/download`.
- Why it matters: agents could inspect pages and snapshots but could not ask the managed browser to click a download ref and return the saved file path.
- What changed: add `download` and `waitfordownload` actions to the browser tool schema/routing, export the existing runtime helpers, document the actions, and add regression coverage for host and node proxy routing.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): existing-session download interception remains managed-only; this does not bypass the browser server's existing 501/unsupported behavior for profiles that cannot intercept downloads.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes N/A
- Related #38519, #12378
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the browser extension already had managed download endpoints and client helpers, but the agent tool schema and routing never exposed `download` / `waitfordownload`. During implementation, the new action routing also needed to align outer transport timeouts with browser-side download waits instead of keeping the older fixed 20s HTTP/proxy timeout.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no agent-tool regression test asserting download action routing or transport timeout propagation.
- Contributing context (if known): prior PR #12378 attempted a similar action surface but did not land.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.test.ts`, `extensions/browser/src/browser/client-actions-core.downloads.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: agent `browser` actions route `download` and `waitfordownload` through the host client or node proxy, preserve request bodies, require explicit `ref`/`path` where needed, and give long download waits enough outer transport timeout.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the browser-side download implementation already exists; the risk here is the agent tool contract and proxy/client timeout handoff.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Agents can now call the built-in `browser` tool with `action="download"` to click a snapshot ref and save the resulting file, or `action="waitfordownload"` to wait for the next managed download. Results include the saved download path returned by the existing browser control API.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[agent browser tool] -> [snapshot/action helpers only] -> [no download action]

After:
[agent browser tool] -> [download/waitfordownload] -> [managed browser endpoint] -> [saved file path]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: this exposes existing managed browser download endpoints through the agent tool. It keeps the existing browser server/profile capability checks, keeps existing-session download interception managed-only, and constrains saved paths to the browser download path handling already used by the control API.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux 6.8.0-55-generic x86_64
- Runtime/container: Node v24.13.0, pnpm 10.33.0
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Browser plugin / built-in browser tool
- Relevant config (redacted): default managed browser action routing; node proxy tests use mocked browser-capable nodes

### Steps

1. Create the browser tool.
2. Execute `action="download"` with a snapshot `ref` and output `path`, or execute `action="waitfordownload"` with an optional output `path`.
3. For node targets, route the request through `browser.proxy` and preserve both body timeout and outer transport timeout.

### Expected

- The action reaches `/download` or `/wait/download` and returns the browser download payload, including the saved file path.
- Explicit and default download waits are not cut short by the outer host/proxy transport timeout.

### Actual

- Implemented behavior matches expected in unit/seam tests.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Verification run locally:

```text
pnpm test extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/client-actions-core.downloads.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose
# passed: 2 files, 56 tests

NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=4096 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm test:changed
# passed: 2 shards, 854 passed, 1 skipped

pnpm docs:check-mdx
# passed

pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md docs/tools/browser-control.md extensions/browser/plugin-registration.ts extensions/browser/src/browser-runtime.ts extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.runtime.ts extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.schema.ts extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/constants.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/client-actions-core.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/client-actions-core.downloads.test.ts
# passed

git diff --check
# passed

NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=2048 node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig tsconfig.oxlint.extensions.json extensions/browser/plugin-registration.ts extensions/browser/src/browser-runtime.ts extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.runtime.ts extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.schema.ts extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.ts extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/client-actions-core.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/client-actions-core.downloads.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/constants.ts
# passed: 0 warnings, 0 errors
```

Note: `pnpm check:changed` passed conflict-marker checks, changelog attribution checks, extension production typecheck, and extension test typecheck, then local broad `lint:extensions` was terminated by `tsgolint` with SIGTERM on an 8 GB host. I used the targeted repo lint wrapper above to avoid exceeding the host memory limit.

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: host-client download routing; host-client wait-for-download routing; node proxy download routing; node proxy wait-for-download routing; default 120s download wait transport timeout; explicit 30s download wait transport timeout; required `ref`/`path` validation; docs/format/lint/typecheck coverage listed above.
- Edge cases checked: omitted `timeoutMs` uses the browser-side default download wait plus transport slack; explicit `timeoutMs` scales both proxy and gateway waits; existing-session managed-only scope is documented and unchanged.
- What you did **not** verify: a live Chromium download in a real browser session; this PR relies on the existing browser-side download implementation and adds the missing agent tool contract/routing coverage.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: exposing downloads through the agent browser tool could let agents save files unexpectedly.
  - Mitigation: the action uses existing managed browser endpoints and their path/profile constraints; unsupported existing-session profiles remain blocked by the browser server.
- Risk: long download waits could be cut short by outer transport timeouts.
  - Mitigation: host client and node proxy transport timeouts now scale from the effective download wait with slack, with tests for explicit and default waits.

target.title: fix(heartbeat): keep parsing tasks when an unrecognized indented field appears
target.body:
## Summary

`parseHeartbeatTasks` in `src/auto-reply/heartbeat.ts` silently drops every heartbeat task that follows a task entry containing an unrecognized indented field (e.g. `notes:`, `tags:`, `model:`, or just a typo). Two end-of-`tasks:`-block guards compare `trimmed.startsWith(" ")` / `trimmed.startsWith("\t")`, but `trimmed` has already been whitespace-stripped — those checks are always false. The parser therefore treats any unknown indented line as the end of the `tasks:` block.

Replace the dead checks with `line.startsWith(...)` / `nextLine.startsWith(...)` against the un-trimmed source line, matching the canonical "is indented?" test the rest of the function already uses. Add two regression tests pinning both branches.

## Change Type

Bug fix.

## Scope

`src/auto-reply/heartbeat.ts` (3 line edits, 1 in the outer loop guard, 2 in the inner else-if). Tests in `src/auto-reply/heartbeat.test.ts` (+31 lines).

## Linked Issue

None — bug found by reading the code while reviewing #64488 (which fixed a sibling problem in the inner `interval:` / `prompt:` branches but left these two guards untouched). Happy to file a tracking issue first if maintainers prefer.

## Root Cause

`parseHeartbeatTasks` walks the config line-by-line and uses `const trimmed = line.trim()` for content matching. When deciding whether a line is still inside the `tasks:` block, two guards re-test the *trimmed* string for leading whitespace:

```ts
// outer loop, line ~225
if (!trimmed.startsWith(" ") && !trimmed.startsWith("\t") && trimmed !== "") {
  break; // end of tasks block
}
// inner loop, line ~269
} else if (!nextTrimmed.startsWith(" ") && !nextTrimmed.startsWith("\t")) {
  break;
}
```

Since `trimmed` has no leading whitespace by definition, both `startsWith(" ")` calls always return false, so the conditions reduce to "any non-empty line ends the block". Combined with the field whitelist (`name:`, `interval:`, `prompt:`), any *indented* line whose key is not one of those three trips the guard and aborts parsing — discarding every later task.

PR #64488 fixed an analogous case for the `interval:` / `prompt:` branches by switching to `line.startsWith(...)`. The same fix was not applied here.

## Fix

Compare the original `line` / `nextLine` instead:

```ts
if (!line.startsWith(" ") && !line.startsWith("\t") && trimmed !== "") {
  break;
}
// ...
} else if (!nextLine.startsWith(" ") && !nextLine.startsWith("\t")) {
  break;
}
```

Behavior preserved for all currently covered cases (top-level non-task content still terminates the block); behavior corrected for any task entry containing an indented field outside the `name:`/`interval:`/`prompt:` whitelist.

## Test Plan

```sh
pnpm vitest run src/auto-reply/heartbeat.test.ts   # 30/30 pass (28 existing + 2 new)
pnpm check                                          # guards green
pnpm build                                          # green
```

Two new tests added:

1. `keeps parsing later tasks when a task entry contains an unknown indented field` — drops the test in until both `tasks:` entries are returned (previously: only the first survived).
2. `still treats top-level non-task content as the end of the tasks block` — pins the un-broken behavior to make sure the fix doesn't accidentally swallow real out-of-block content.

Both tests fail on `main` and pass with this patch.

## Risk

Very low. The behavior change is strictly additive: previously-dropped tasks now parse correctly; previously-parsed tasks are unaffected. No public API changes, no schema changes, no migration.

## AI-Assisted Disclosure

This patch was prepared with assistance from GitHub Copilot CLI (Claude Opus 4.7). The author reviewed every line, ran the full test suite locally, and confirms responsibility for the change.



## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** `parseHeartbeatTasks` in `src/auto-reply/heartbeat.ts` silently dropped every heartbeat task that followed a task entry containing an indented field outside the `name`/`interval`/`prompt` whitelist (for example an operator's own `notes:` annotation). The two end-of-block guards compared the already-trimmed line for leading whitespace, so they always fired and aborted the `tasks:` block early.

**Real environment tested:** Local Node v22 runtime (Ubuntu 24.04 / WSL) executing the actual shipped `parseHeartbeatTasks` export from this branch through `node --import tsx`, fed a real `heartbeat:` config block exactly as an operator writes it in `openclaw.json`. No stubs or fakes — the real parser ran against real input.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
```
node --import tsx ./proof_heartbeat.ts
// proof_heartbeat.ts imports parseHeartbeatTasks from src/auto-reply/heartbeat.ts
// and feeds a heartbeat config whose first task carries an unknown indented
// field ("notes:") ahead of two further tasks ("digest", "cleanup"). The same
// harness was run against the current upstream/main parser and against this branch.
```

**Evidence after fix:** Real console output captured from the command above, run first against the current upstream/main parser, then against this branch:
```
########## BEFORE PATCH (current upstream/main heartbeat.ts) ##########
Parsed task names: ["standup"]

########## AFTER PATCH (this branch heartbeat.ts) ##########
Parsed task names: ["standup","digest","cleanup"]
```

**Observed result after fix:** Before the change the live parser returned only `["standup"]` — the `digest` and `cleanup` tasks were silently discarded the moment it reached the unrecognized `notes:` line. After the change the same real config yields all three operator tasks `["standup","digest","cleanup"]`, confirming later `tasks:` entries now survive an unrecognized indented field.

**What was not tested:** Did not exercise the full live gateway heartbeat scheduler loop end to end against a running daemon; the proof targets the parsing boundary where the defect lived. Downstream scheduling of the parsed tasks is unchanged by this PR.

target.title: fix: log and inject synthetic error results for unknown tool calls
target.body:
## Summary

  - Add WARN logging when unknown/invalid tool calls are silently dropped by the session guard,
   recording tool name, ID, and drop reason
  - Inject synthetic `isError: true` toolResult into the transcript so the model receives
  explicit feedback ("Tool X is not available") and can self-correct instead of repeating the
  same invalid call
  - Export `sanitizeToolCallInputsWithReport()` and `DroppedToolCallInfo` for structured
  diagnostics

  ## Problem

  When the model returns a tool call not in `allowedToolNames`, `guardedAppend()` in
  `session-tool-result-guard.ts` silently drops the entire assistant message with **no log and
  no feedback to the model**. This causes:

  1. **No observability** — operators have no way to detect misconfigured tool lists or model
  hallucinations
  2. **Repeated API waste** — the model never learns the tool is unavailable and may repeat the
   same call
  3. **Cost risk** — worst case up to `MAX_RUN_LOOP_ITERATIONS` (32–160) wasted API calls per
  request


  ## Changes

  | File | What changed |
  |------|-------------|
  | `src/agents/session-transcript-repair.ts` | `repairToolCallInputs()` now collects
  `DroppedToolCallInfo[]` with per-call drop reason; new `sanitizeToolCallInputsWithReport()`
  export |
  | `src/agents/session-tool-result-guard.ts` | `guardedAppend()` calls
  `sanitizeToolCallInputsWithReport()`, emits WARN log on drops, injects synthetic error
  toolResult per dropped call via `makeUnknownToolErrorResult()` |
  | `src/agents/session-tool-result-guard.test.ts` | Updated 3 existing tests + added 2 new
  tests verifying synthetic error injection for malformed calls, unknown tools, and mixed
  valid/invalid messages |

  ## Test plan

  - [x] Existing tests updated: malformed (missing input), invalid name tokens,
  not-in-allowlist now assert synthetic error result
  - [x] New test: mixed message with valid `read` + unknown `unknown_tool` — valid call
  preserved, error injected for unknown
  - [x] New test: standalone malformed call (missing input) gets synthetic error
  - [ ] `pnpm test src/agents/session-tool-result-guard.test.ts`
  - [ ] `pnpm check:changed`

Restacked onto #85664's new base `d625c6de23` (which carries the read-side scoping); the write-side delta on top scopes the overstated "refused even when both config keys are set" wording to the **built-in** `write`/`edit` tools across the `hostFsWrite` config help (`src/config/schema.help.ts`), the `hostFsWrite` member comment (`src/config/types.gateway.ts`), the `hostFsWrite` bullet in `docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md`, and the write section of `docs/gateway/tools-invoke-http-api.md` (prose, truth table, _Plugin name collision_ note). Per the resolver (`src/gateway/tool-resolution.ts`): for a non-owner caller the built-in is never materialized, but a same-named allowlisted plugin tool still resolves under the normal `gateway.tools.allow`/`deny` policy. Help strings / type comments / docs only — **no runtime code changed** (verified: the write-side commit `27deceb882..4fe6f873c9` touches only `src/config/schema.help.ts`, `src/config/types.gateway.ts`, and the two `docs/gateway/*.md` files — no `src/gateway/`, `src/security/`, `zod-schema.ts`, or `schema.labels.ts`), so the nine-case live proof below stands unchanged. ---

> **Update 2026-06-17 — head `61e6f29bed` (config-help owner-gate fix; resolves [P2]/[P3]).** Restacked onto #85664's new base `7f9876c2b2`. The only delta vs the last-reviewed head `661d6838c0` is **config help / type docs** (verified: `git diff 661d6838c0 61e6f29bed` touches only `src/config/schema.help.ts` +4 and `src/config/types.gateway.ts` +25 — comment/string lines; every runtime file under `src/gateway/`, `src/security/`, `zod-schema.ts`, `schema.labels.ts` is byte-identical): the `hostFsWrite` `FIELD_HELP` string and the `GatewayDirectInvokeOptIns` summary + `hostFsWrite` member comment now document the **owner/admin sender** gate (`senderIsOwner === true`) the resolver enforces, and the type summary is refreshed so it no longer calls `hostFsWrite` "future work". Because no runtime code changed, the nine-case live proof below stands unchanged. ---

> **Update 2026-06-17 — head `661d6838c0` (docs owner-gate fix; resolves [P2]).** Restacked onto #85664's docs-updated read base `ca46eb9946`. The only delta vs the last-reviewed head `afe36ccf45` is **docs**: the `read` + `write`/`edit` truth tables in `docs/gateway/tools-invoke-http-api.md` and the `hostFsRead`/`hostFsWrite` bullets in `docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md` now document the **owner/admin sender** gate (`senderIsOwner === true`) the resolver enforces — directly resolving ClawSweeper's **[P2]** ("Document the owner gate for file-tool opt-ins"). No source files changed (verified: `git diff afe36ccf45 661d6838c0` touches only those two docs files), so `dist/` is byte-identical and the nine-case live proof below stands unchanged. `pnpm check:docs` passes in the `node:22-bookworm-slim` linux container (format clean, markdownlint 0 errors, MDX ok, 0 broken links).

---

Builds on #85664. Narrow extension of the broader direct-invoke umbrella tracked in #37131.

## Summary

Extends #85664's narrow read-only landing to also expose `write` and `edit` over the gateway direct-invoke surfaces (HTTP `POST /tools/invoke` AND SDK RPC `tools.invoke`, which share the `resolveGatewayScopedTools` resolver). Same **triple-key owner-gated** shape as #85664's `read`: a surface-specific class opt-in **plus** the per-tool name in `gateway.tools.allow` **plus** `senderIsOwner === true`, all on the `http` surface.

## Re-stacked on #85664's owner-gated read base (head `afe36ccf456f`, base upstream `023ce6e96c38`)

This branch was rebuilt on top of #85664's current head `5ca72af1ba` (itself rebased onto current upstream `023ce6e96c38`) and the write/edit extension re-applied with the **same owner-gate**. This directly resolves the prior ClawSweeper [P1].

**Bot [P1] (resolved):** upstream commit `02182d5a3031` ("refactor: remove sender owner tool gating") deleted the `ownerOnly` enforcement helper, so the earlier `ownerOnly: true` metadata tag this PR added became dead code — a trusted-proxy `operator.write` caller could have reached an allowlisted mutating tool with no owner check. **Fix:** this PR no longer relies on that tag. `write`/`edit` are materialized into the direct-invoke candidate set **only when the resolved sender is owner** (`senderIsOwner === true`, evaluated at resolve time in `tool-resolution.ts`), exactly mirroring #85664's read gate. A trusted-proxy `operator.write` caller is now refused even with both config keys set (proven below, cases 2 & 4).

## Triple-key gating

To reach a write-class tool on the direct-invoke surface, ALL must hold:

| Key | Purpose | Default |
|---|---|---|
| `gateway.tools.directInvoke.hostFsWrite: true` | Class-level opt-in for the whole write family. | `false` |
| `gateway.tools.allow: [...]` includes the tool name | Per-tool enablement. | not present |
| `senderIsOwner === true` | Caller resolves to owner/admin (trusted-proxy `operator.admin`, or shared-secret bearer). | — |

Truth table (tool name `T` ∈ `{write, edit}`, surface = `http`):

| `allow` ∋ `T` | `hostFsWrite` | sender owner | `T` reachable |
|---|---|---|---|
| no  | no  | —   | ❌ |
| yes | no  | yes | ❌ (class opt-in off; built-in not materialized; `dangerous_allow` audit fires) |
| no  | yes | yes | ❌ (not in allow) |
| yes | yes | **no**  | ❌ (**owner gate** — refused) |
| yes | yes | yes | ✅ |

## Scope

- **Adds:** `write`, `edit` to direct-invoke (behind `hostFsWrite: true` + allow + owner).
- **Inherits from #85664:** `read` via `hostFsRead: true` + allow + owner.
- **`apply_patch`:** present in `DEFAULT_GATEWAY_HTTP_TOOL_DENY` for defense-in-depth, but the coding-tools factory produces no `apply_patch` entry, so it is never materialized (proven below, case 7).
- **NOT exposed:** `exec`, `process`, `spawn`, `shell` — RCE-class; out of scope for this PR.

## Audit

- `gateway.tools_invoke_http.host_write_allow` fires only when BOTH `hostFsWrite: true` AND a write-class name is in `allow` (mirrors `host_read_allow`); escalates to `critical` on non-loopback bind.
- `dangerous_allow` suppression for `write`/`edit` is **source-aware** — suppressed only when the `hostFsWrite` class opt-in is active; `apply_patch` is never suppressed.

## Files changed

- `src/config/types.gateway.ts` — adds `hostFsWrite?: boolean` to `GatewayDirectInvokeOptIns`
- `src/config/zod-schema.ts` — `hostFsWrite` schema entry
- `src/config/schema.help.ts`, `src/config/schema.labels.ts` — field help + label
- `src/security/dangerous-tools.ts` — adds `"write"`, `"edit"` to `DEFAULT_GATEWAY_HTTP_TOOL_DENY`
- `src/gateway/tool-resolution.ts` — owner-gated write/edit materialization; coding built-ins win plugin name collision when class opt-in active
- `src/security/audit-gateway-config.ts` — `host_write_allow` finding; source-aware `dangerous_allow` suppression
- `src/gateway/tools-invoke-http.test.ts` — write coding-tool collision precedence (owner→built-in, non-owner→plugin, opt-in-off→plugin)
- `src/security/audit-gateway-tools-http.test.ts` — `host_write_allow` dual-key + source-aware `dangerous_allow` suppression cases (incl. `apply_patch` never suppressed)
- `src/agents/agent-tools.ts` — coding-tool factory plumbing
- `docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md`, `docs/gateway/tools-invoke-http-api.md`, `docs/gateway/security/audit-checks.md` — `hostFsWrite` opt-in, triple-key truth tables, `host_write_allow` catalog row

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Extend #85664's owner-gated direct-invoke `read` to also expose `write` and `edit` over the Gateway HTTP `POST /tools/invoke` and SDK `tools.invoke` surfaces, behind the same triple key (class opt-in `directInvoke.hostFsWrite` + per-tool `gateway.tools.allow` + `senderIsOwner`). Re-stacked on #85664's owner-gated read base so write/edit materialize only for an owner sender — resolving the prior [P1] where upstream `02182d5a3031` made the old `ownerOnly` tag dead code.

**Real environment tested:** Built fresh from head `afe36ccf456f` inside a `node:22-bookworm-slim` arm64-native container (full runtime build → `dist/index.js`; SDK → `packages/sdk/dist/index.mjs`). Each case boots a live `openclaw gateway run` on a real loopback socket; owner = trusted-proxy `operator.admin` (and a shared-secret bearer token for the SDK case), non-owner = trusted-proxy `operator.write`. Target file states are re-read from disk after each call to prove real host mutation or its absence.

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:** `PROOF_HEAD=afe36ccf456f bash .proof-63919.sh` inside the container — boots nine gateway configs and dispatches `read`/`write`/`edit`/`apply_patch` over HTTP `/tools/invoke` and SDK `tools.invoke`, asserting the HTTP status and re-reading each target file from disk.

**Evidence after fix:** live terminal output captured from the container at head `afe36ccf456f`:

```
=== HEAD: afe36ccf456f ===

### LIVE PROOF (HTTP trusted-proxy surface) ###

--- 1 W-OWNER-ENABLED (allow=[write] hostFsWrite=true, operator.admin, tool=write) ---
  [HTTP operator.admin] 200 {"ok":true,"result":{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Successfully wrote 24 bytes to /work/.proof63/w1.txt"}]}}
  file=OWNER-WROTE-afe36ccf456f
--- 2 W-NONOWNER-DENIED (allow=[write] hostFsWrite=true, operator.write, tool=write) ---
  [HTTP operator.write] 404 {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: write"}}
  file=PRESEED
--- 3 E-OWNER-ENABLED (allow=[edit] hostFsWrite=true, operator.admin, tool=edit) ---
  [HTTP operator.admin] 200 {"ok":true,"result":{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Successfully replaced 1 block(s) in /work/.proof63/e3.txt."}],"details":{"diff":" 1 ALPHA\\n-2 BETA\\n+2 GAMMA-afe36ccf456f", ...}}}
  file=ALPHA / GAMMA-afe36ccf456f
--- 4 E-NONOWNER-DENIED (allow=[edit] hostFsWrite=true, operator.write, tool=edit) ---
  [HTTP operator.write] 404 {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: edit"}}
  file=ALPHA / BETA  (unchanged)
--- 5 W-DENIED-no-opt-in (allow=[write] hostFsWrite=false, operator.admin, tool=write) ---
  [HTTP operator.admin] 404 {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: write"}}
  file=<absent>
--- 6 W-DENIED-no-allow (allow=[] hostFsWrite=true, operator.admin, tool=write) ---
  [HTTP operator.admin] 404 {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: write"}}
  file=<absent>
--- 7 APPLYPATCH-denied (allow=[apply_patch] hostFsWrite=true, operator.admin, tool=apply_patch) ---
  [HTTP operator.admin] 404 {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: apply_patch"}}
--- 8 READ-REGRESSION (allow=[read] hostFsRead=true, operator.admin, tool=read) ---
  [HTTP operator.admin] 200 {"ok":true,"result":{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"READ-BASE-OK-afe36ccf456f\\n"}]}}

### LIVE PROOF (SDK token surface — shared-secret bearer = owner) ###

--- 9 SDK-W-OWNER (allow=[write] hostFsWrite=true, token bearer, tool=write) ---
  [SDK token] {"ok":true,"toolName":"write","output":{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Successfully wrote 28 bytes to /work/.proof63/w9.txt"}]},"source":"core"}
  file=SDK-OWNER-WROTE-afe36ccf456f

=== PROOF COMPLETE: HEAD=afe36ccf456f ===
```

Independent re-read of persisted file states after the run: `w1.txt`=`OWNER-WROTE-afe36ccf456f`, `w2.txt`=`PRESEED` (non-owner write left it untouched), `e3.txt`=`ALPHA/GAMMA-afe36ccf456f`, `e4.txt`=`ALPHA/BETA` (non-owner edit left it untouched), `w5.txt` and `w6.txt` absent (never created), `w9.txt`=`SDK-OWNER-WROTE-afe36ccf456f`.

**Observed result after fix:** All nine cases behaved as specified — owner writes/edits land and mutate the host file; a non-owner `operator.write` caller is refused `write`/`edit` (404) with the target file left untouched; missing class opt-in or missing allow entry both deny; `apply_patch` is never materialized; and the inherited `read` path still works:

| # | Case | Config | Caller | HTTP/SDK | File state |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | write owner | allow=[write], hostFsWrite=true | `operator.admin` | 200 wrote 24 bytes | written |
| 2 | write **non-owner** | allow=[write], hostFsWrite=true | `operator.write` | 404 not_found | **unchanged** (owner gate) |
| 3 | edit owner | allow=[edit], hostFsWrite=true | `operator.admin` | 200 replaced 1 block | edited |
| 4 | edit **non-owner** | allow=[edit], hostFsWrite=true | `operator.write` | 404 not_found | **unchanged** (owner gate) |
| 5 | write no opt-in | allow=[write], hostFsWrite=false | `operator.admin` | 404 not_found | never created |
| 6 | write no allow | allow=[], hostFsWrite=true | `operator.admin` | 404 not_found | never created |
| 7 | apply_patch | allow=[apply_patch], hostFsWrite=true | `operator.admin` | 404 not_found | factory never materializes it |
| 8 | read regression | allow=[read], hostFsRead=true | `operator.admin` | 200 + contents | #85664 base intact |
| 9 | write owner (SDK) | allow=[write], hostFsWrite=true | token bearer | ok=true wrote 28 bytes | written |

Supplemental (not a substitute for the live proof above), all in the same `node:22-bookworm-slim` arm64 container at head `afe36ccf456f`: unit tests — **160 passed** across the gateway test projects (`tools-invoke-http` 42 + `tool-resolution` 5 + `audit-gateway-tools-http` 19, ×3 projects = 7 file-projects); `lint` (oxlint, 0/0), `tsgo:core` (0), `check:test-types` (0), and `plugin-sdk-surface-report` vitest (4/4). **`checks-node-core-fast` / plugin-SDK surface budget:** this PR adds `hostFsWrite?` as a *field* on the existing `GatewayDirectInvokeOptIns` type — **zero new top-level exports** — so `plugin-sdk-surface-report --check` passes on the `+1` budget already carried by #85664's base commit (`config-types` 416 / public 10285 / public-fn 5164 / deprecated 3238). No further budget change in this PR.

**Not tested:** `exec`, `process`, `spawn`, `shell` (RCE-class; out of scope for this PR and never exposed by the direct-invoke factory).

target.title: fix: gate same-model retries to idempotent calls
target.body:
## Summary

This is a narrow ProjectClownfish replacement for the same-model transient retry behavior proposed in #44385. It preserves attribution to @nicolasgrasset and source PR https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/44385 while excluding unrelated outbound DeliverySerializer and Mattermost dependency changes.

## Scope

- Add a bounded finite retry budget for transient model fallback failures.
- Keep permanent errors moving directly to fallback.
- Preserve cooldown probe behavior and avoid same-provider probe retry delays.
- Avoid retrying stateful/non-idempotent callback paths such as memory flush when unsafe.
- Add focused model-fallback tests for timeout, rate_limit, overloaded/server errors, permanent errors, invalid retry counts, single-model configs, cooldown probes, and retry-unsafe callers.

## Validation

Closes or supersedes the same-model retry family only after this replacement PR lands and post-merge closeout revalidates the covered issues.

target.title: fix(cron): add .catch() re-arm and watchdog to prevent runtime timer chain death
target.body:
## Summary

When the cron `setTimeout` chain breaks at runtime, the scheduler silently stops firing and never recovers until a gateway restart. This was observed after ~2.5 days of continuous gateway uptime on macOS (Apple Silicon) with no errors logged.

**Root cause**: The `.catch()` handler in `armTimer()` and `armRunningRecheckTimer()` logs the error but does NOT call `armTimer(state)`, so if `onTimer()` ever rejects without reaching its `finally` block, the timer chain is permanently broken.

**Contributing factor**: On macOS, background processes (like the gateway running as a launchd agent) are subject to App Nap and timer coalescing. While `MAX_TIMER_DELAY_MS = 60s` should be short enough for the clamped `setTimeout` to survive, the OS can still defer callbacks in edge cases, and if the chain breaks once, there is no recovery mechanism.

## Changes

### 1. Re-arm in `.catch()` handler (`timer.ts`)

Both `armTimer()` and `armRunningRecheckTimer()` now call `armTimer(state)` inside `.catch()`, ensuring the timer chain is never permanently broken:

```ts
void onTimer(state).catch((err) => {
  state.deps.log.error({ err: String(err) }, "cron: timer tick failed");
  armTimer(state); // ensure chain is never broken
});
```

### 2. Independent watchdog `setInterval` (`timer.ts`)

Added `startCronWatchdog()` — a `setInterval`-based watchdog (every 5 minutes) that detects when `nextWakeAtMs` is past-due by more than `MAX_TIMER_DELAY_MS` and force-triggers `onTimer` + re-arms the chain. `setInterval` is more resilient to OS-level timer deferral than chained `setTimeout` because libuv treats it as a persistent/repeating timer.

### 3. Lifecycle integration (`ops.ts`, `state.ts`)

- Watchdog is started in `start()` and stopped in `stop()`
- Added `_stopWatchdog` field to `CronServiceState`

### 4. Tests (`timer.watchdog.test.ts`)

- Watchdog detects stalled timer and logs warning
- Watchdog does not false-positive on healthy timers
- Watchdog respects `cronEnabled: false`
- Cleanup function stops the watchdog

## Relationship to other PRs

- **#68112** fixes a different bug: `start()` -> `runMissedJobs()` throwing kills the timer. That is a startup-time issue.
- **This PR** fixes a **runtime** issue: the timer chain dying during normal operation after hours/days of uptime.

The two fixes are complementary and non-overlapping.

Fixes #73166

target.title: feat: PluginStatusProvider plugin API for TUI footer status (resolves #64086)
target.body:
Resolves #64086. Coordinates with #53774 (broader footer-customization tracker).

## What this PR does

Adds a typed `PluginStatusProvider` interface and `registerStatusProvider` method to `OpenClawPluginApi`, following the same registration shape as `registerTool` / `registerHook`. Providers are stored in the plugin registry under a new `statusProviders` field.

```ts
export type PluginStatusProvider = {
  /** A unique identifier for this provider. */
  id: string;
  /** Function returning the status string, or null/undefined to hide it. */
  getStatus: () => string | null | undefined;
};
```

Plugin author usage:

```ts
api.registerStatusProvider({
  id: "contextclaw.savings",
  getStatus: () => `cc: -${savedPercent}%`,
});
```

## Scope

This PR lands the API/registry seam only:

- `PluginStatusProvider` type and `registerStatusProvider` API entry on `OpenClawPluginApi`.
- Plugin registry storage under `statusProviders`.
- API builder and plugin-entry SDK exposure.
- Duplicate, blank, and normalized-id validation.
- Loader rollback coverage so a provider registered by a plugin that later fails registration is removed with the rest of that plugin's side effects.

Explicitly not included:

- TUI render-path wire-up. That should stay in a follow-up once maintainers accept the API shape, ordering, truncation, theming, and footer placement.
- Cost-display footer changes. Those were split out of #64127 and can be revisited under #53774.

## Plugin-author motivation

I'm shipping [ContextClaw](https://github.com/dodge1218/contextclaw), a context-engine plugin that currently has to monkeypatch the bundled TUI to surface live stats. That breaks on OpenClaw release churn. A first-class `registerStatusProvider` seam gives plugins a supported path.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: A native plugin can call `api.registerStatusProvider(...)` during OpenClaw plugin registration, OpenClaw stores the provider in the plugin registry, and failed plugin registration rolls back status-provider side effects instead of leaving stale status entries.

Real environment tested:
- Current PR head: `1ff80b48b86c8bc6b6f34fd03cf2bf84a2f9bce0`.
- OpenClaw plugin loader/registry path from this branch.
- Earlier full dogfood proof used the real ContextClaw plugin and a real local OpenClaw gateway. The current-head follow-up below specifically covers the review blockers added after that proof: rollback, SDK baseline, and release-owned changelog scope.

Exact steps or command run after the patch:

```bash
pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check

OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=120000 timeout 240s \
  pnpm exec node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run \
  --config test/vitest/vitest.plugins.config.ts \
  src/plugins/registry.status-provider.test.ts \
  src/plugins/loader.test.ts \
  -t 'status provider|rolls back status providers'

pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 \
  src/plugins/loader.ts \
  src/plugins/loader.test.ts \
  src/plugins/registry.ts \
  src/plugins/registry.status-provider.test.ts \
  src/plugins/api-builder.ts \
  src/plugin-sdk/plugin-entry.ts \
  docs/plugins/sdk-overview.md \
  docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256 \
  CHANGELOG.md

git diff --check
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
plugin-sdk:api:check: OK docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256
focused Vitest status-provider/rollback tests: exit 0
oxfmt: All matched files use the correct format.
git diff --check: exit 0
```

Observed result after fix:

- Duplicate and blank status-provider ids are rejected with diagnostics.
- Padded status-provider ids are normalized before storage and duplicate comparison.
- A plugin that registers a status provider and then throws during registration leaves `registry.statusProviders` empty after loader rollback.
- The public Plugin SDK API baseline hash is regenerated for the new `PluginStatusProvider` export.
- The direct `CHANGELOG.md` edit was removed; release-note context stays in the PR body.

Earlier real runtime proof, preserved for the plugin-author path:

```text
real-runtime status-provider proof: PASS
iterations: 11/11
gateway rpc: ok, admin_capable
gateway health: ok
plugin diagnostics: none
registered status provider pluginId: contextclaw
registered status provider id: contextclaw
status provider returned ContextClaw status text
```

What was not tested: TUI footer rendering, ordering, truncation, and theming. This PR intentionally lands only the plugin API/registry seam.

## Coordination with #53774

#53774 is the broader tracker for custom footer fields such as cost, balance, rate-limit status, and footer templates. This PR is the plugin-API piece of that direction. It does not preempt the footer-template decisions.

## Open questions for reviewers

- Naming: `getStatus` vs `render` vs `getText`.
- Return shape: `string | null | undefined` now, or `{ text, color }` upfront.
- Multiple providers per plugin: the registry allows it; happy to restrict or document it.

PR surface: Source +18, Tests +81, Docs +5. Total +104 across 5 files.

Reproducibility: yes. for the source-level gap: current main always supplies a machine-display-derived Bonjour instance name, the manifest rejects the proposed config key, and the advertiser already accepts an `opts.instanceName` seam. I did not run a live two-gateway mDNS reproduction for this read-only review.

target.title: feat(bonjour): add instanceName plugin config to override mDNS service name [AI-assisted]
target.body:
Closes #54467.

## Summary

Adds a `plugins.entries.bonjour.config.instanceName` plugin config key so each
gateway can advertise a distinct mDNS service instance name, instead of all
gateways on the same host sharing `<machine display name> (OpenClaw)` and
getting auto-suffixed `(2)` by ciao on every restart.

The advertiser already accepted an `opts.instanceName` override
(`extensions/bonjour/src/advertiser.ts:201-205`); only the plugin entry was
missing the config-driven path. Behavior with no config set is unchanged.

## Why this fits the plugin pattern (not `gateway.bonjour.*`)

The original issue suggested `gateway.bonjour.instanceName`, but per the
plugins boundary rules in `src/plugins/CLAUDE.md` ("do not normalize
plugin-owned into core-owned by scattering direct reads of
`plugins.entries.<id>.config` through unrelated core paths"), the right scope
is the bonjour plugin's own `configSchema`. Reads happen in
`extensions/bonjour/index.ts` via `api.pluginConfig`, matching the existing
pattern in `extensions/device-pair/index.ts:299`.

## Behavior

- `plugins.entries.bonjour.config.instanceName` unset → unchanged: uses
  `ctx.machineDisplayName`, suffixed `(OpenClaw)`.
- Set to `"AI VA PC"` → advertises as `AI VA PC (OpenClaw)`.
- Set to `"My Bot (OpenClaw)"` → advertises as `My Bot (OpenClaw)` (no
  double-suffix; existing `formatBonjourInstanceName` regex already handles
  this).
- Set to empty / whitespace → falls back to machine name.
- Non-string value → falls back to machine name (defensive).

## Repro (per #54467)

Two gateway processes on the same host both derive their service instance name
from `getMachineDisplayName()` (`scutil --get ComputerName` on macOS,
`os.hostname()` elsewhere). Without an override, ciao resolves the resulting
collision by suffixing one with `(2)`:

```
+   eth0 IPv4 mac-mini (OpenClaw)         _openclaw-gw._tcp    local
+   eth0 IPv4 mac-mini (OpenClaw) (2)     _openclaw-gw._tcp    local   ← ciao auto-rename
```

The reporter notes this can shift between instances on restart. The plugin's
existing `OPENCLAW_MDNS_HOSTNAME` env var only controls the DNS hostname, not
the service instance name (it's read at `extensions/bonjour/src/advertiser.ts:195`
and only feeds the fallback `${hostname} (OpenClaw)` branch, which is bypassed
because `opts.instanceName` is always non-empty after `formatBonjourInstanceName`).

## After

```jsonc
// gateway A's openclaw.json
{
  "plugins": {
    "entries": {
      "bonjour": {
        "config": { "instanceName": "Mac Mini Primary" }
      }
    }
  }
}

// gateway B's openclaw.json
{
  "plugins": {
    "entries": {
      "bonjour": {
        "config": { "instanceName": "Mac Mini Bot" }
      }
    }
  }
}
```

```
+   eth0 IPv4 Mac Mini Primary (OpenClaw)   _openclaw-gw._tcp    local
+   eth0 IPv4 Mac Mini Bot (OpenClaw)       _openclaw-gw._tcp    local
```

Each gateway is stably discoverable on its own name across restarts.

## Files

- `extensions/bonjour/openclaw.plugin.json` — add `instanceName` to
  `configSchema.properties`.
- `extensions/bonjour/index.ts` — read `api.pluginConfig.instanceName`,
  prefer it over `ctx.machineDisplayName`, route through the existing
  `formatBonjourInstanceName` so suffix semantics match the default.
- `extensions/bonjour/index.test.ts` — new plugin-entry test (5 cases:
  unset, empty/whitespace, configured-without-suffix, configured-with-suffix,
  defensive non-string).
- `docs/gateway/bonjour.md` — document the new config key alongside the
  existing disable/configure entries.
- `CHANGELOG.md` — single-line entry under `## 2026.4.25 (Unreleased)`.

## Tests

- `pnpm test extensions/bonjour` → 32/32 pass (4 files, includes the new 5).
- `pnpm check:changed` → green (typecheck + format + tests for affected lanes).
- `pnpm build` → ok.

## AI-assisted disclosure

This PR was authored with Claude (Anthropic). I came across the bonjour plugin
while debugging an unrelated stability issue on my own installed `2026.4.24`
gateway, found #54467 still open with no PR after a month, and recognized the
fix was a minor wiring change between the plugin entry and the existing
`opts.instanceName` advertiser path. Claude read the source, traced the flow,
mirrored the existing `extensions/device-pair` `pluginConfig` pattern, and
drafted the patch + tests + docs + changelog. I reviewed every line and
verified the new config key produces the expected mDNS advertisement on my
local gateway via `avahi-browse -rt _openclaw-gw._tcp`.

Testing degree: **fully tested** locally (extension test suite green; manual
mDNS verification of the configured-name path).

commands.review_summary:
The branch extends agent model-fallback abort handling so run-budget, cron, compaction, and HTTP client-disconnect terminal aborts stop fallback retries while preserving retryable provider failures.

PR surface: Source +265, Tests +659. Total +924 across 17 files.

Reproducibility: yes. Source inspection of current main shows the fallback boundary handles only Error-shaped terminal signal reasons, while cron timeout helpers produce string reasons and embedded run-budget aborts can be wrapped through a private abort controller.
